The test helper GetProfileLogID (test code running from main) searches for a log regex (Ptp4lConf: #profile: ) that is only emitted after *configInput = configOutput + printNodeProfile — code introduced in linuxptp-daemon main PR #617 (commit fd6ff1e, merged 2026-06-30) and not backported to release-4.20. The CI job deploys release-4.20 daemon images (USE_UPSTREAM=false), so the pattern is never logged. GetProfileLogID's internal 3-minute deadline consumes the entire outer Eventually window, leaving the /var/run fallback no time to run. All failures surface as either timedout waiting for matches (stream alive, pattern never appeared) or context deadline exceeded (Kubernetes log-stream dropped), with a secondary cluster-API Unauthorized state observed post-run in dualnicbc/dualnicbcha, consistent with infrastructure stress from 11 concurrent 200-second stream-read timeouts.


• 2/13 failures — T-GM DPLL does not enter holdover on GNSS disruption (tgm: 2)

When GNSS signal is lost or rebooted on WPC T-GM hardware, the Intel DPLL is expected to transition to lockStatus: holdover (clock class 7). Instead, the DPLL stays in or falls directly to FREERUN (clock class 248), so openshift_ptp_clock_class never reaches 7. Both tgm tests fail: one by timeout polling for clock class 7, one by direct metric assertion finding 248. This is independent of the log-line issue; the release-4.20 daemon or underlying firmware may have a regression in holdover state management.



Cross-Suite Patterns:


Root CauseSuites AffectedFailures
#profile: log line missing from release-4.20 daemonbc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha11
T-GM DPLL does not enter holdover (clock class 7)tgm2

The 11-failure group is fully cross-suite: all four PTP topology suites hit identical pod-log-stream errors on six different pods across different nodes, ruling out any single pod, node, or suite-specific cause. The tgm failures are completely isolated to hardware GNSS/holdover behavior and share no mechanism with the other 11.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Backport linuxptp-daemon main PR #617 to release-4.20 (fixes 11/13 failures). The commit fd6ff1e adds *configInput = configOutput and the unconditional printNodeProfile call that emits Ptp4lConf: #profile: into daemon logs. Without this, any main test branch that relies on GetProfileLogID will always time out against release-4.20 images. Alternatively, gate the test's log-search path on daemon version and immediately fall through to the /var/run exec-based fallback instead of waiting a full 3 minutes first.

2. Investigate T-GM DPLL holdover regression on release-4.20 (fixes 2/13 failures). Audit recent commits to linuxptp-daemon/release-4.20 and cloud-event-proxy/release-4.20 for changes in pkg/event/event.go DPLL state-transition logic. Also verify WPC hardware firmware version matches the expected holdover capability; if firmware is the variable, pin firmware version in the CI telco5g cluster.

3. Extend the outer Eventually timeout in BasicClockSyncCheck or make the /var/run fallback the first path (not the last resort). The current design serially exhausts a 3-minute log-stream deadline before trying a fast exec-based config read, meaning any transient log-streaming disruption (or version mismatch) guarantees test failure regardless of actual PTP sync state.


Overall Issue Class: test_regression

(The dominant failure mode — 11/13 — is caused by main test code depending on a daemon log line that exists only in main, not in the release-4.20 images the job is actually testing. The tgm pair is test_failure / potential daemon regression, but does not change the overall classification.)


Overall Severity: HIGH

(11/13 failures are systemic and reproducible across every topology suite in every run that pairs main test scripts with release-4.20 daemon images; the job cannot pass in this configuration without the backport or a test-side workaround.)




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 BC ClockSync tests time out because GetProfileLogID cannot find the Ptp4lConf: #profile: log pattern in the release-4.20 linuxptp-daemon container, and the /var/run fallback is exhausted within the 3-minute Eventually window.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID log pattern absent from release-4.20 linuxptp-daemon (affects: all 3 tests — "Slave can sync to master", "Downstream slave can sync to BC master", "Can provide a profile with higher priority")
• Evidence:

```

could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-g59s2 container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

```

could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-7khz9 container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

```

could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-g59s2 container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=timedout waiting for matches

```

The same error occurs across all four suites (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha) on different pods (-g59s2, -7khz9, -68lc9, -d75dq), confirming a daemon-wide, systematic failure.


• Root Cause: GetClockIDForeign (called from BasicClockSyncCheck in test/pkg/ptptesthelper/ptptesthelper.go:66) first calls GetProfileLogID (test/pkg/ptphelper/ptphelper.go:84), which searches for the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\] in the linuxptp-daemon-container logs. This pattern is only emitted when the linuxptp-daemon calls printNodeProfile(nodeProfile) after configInput = configOutput (linuxptp-daemon/pkg/daemon/daemon.go:1152-1154) writes the rendered config (containing #profile: ) back into nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf. Both the *configInput = configOutput assignment and the unconditional printNodeProfile call at line 1412 were introduced in linuxptp-daemon main PR #617 (commit fd6ff1e, merged June 30 2026), but have not been backported to release-4.20. The CI job runs test scripts from ptp-operator main against release-4.20 production images, so the daemon never emits the expected log line. The /var/run-based fallback (GetConfigForProfileFromVarRun, added in ptp-operator main commit 84723c8 June 22) is invoked only after GetProfileLogID's own 3-minute deadline elapses (~t=200s), which coincides with the outer Eventually timeout (3 min), leaving insufficient time to complete exec commands inside the pod and verify the result. GetClockIDForeign then returns lastErr from the log scan, and Eventually reports failure at ~202s.

• Breaking PR/Commit: linuxptp-daemon main PR #617 (fd6ff1e, June 30 2026) added *configInput = configOutput and the unconditional printNodeProfile that the test requires — but this has not been cherry-picked to release-4.20. Per branch-awareness policy, a main-only change cannot be the root cause; the true underlying issue is that the main test code (GetProfileLogID, introduced in ptp-operator commit 73a75ec, March 25 2026) was written assuming a log-output behavior that exists only on main linuxptp-daemon, not in release-4.20 images. Secondary contributing commit: ptp-operator bb43e35 (June 23 2026) has prerequisite k8snetworkplumbingwg/linuxptp-daemon#215 which also affects daemon startup behavior
2026-07-01T05:00
❌ failure4.214.21-e2e-telco5g-ptp296mtest_flake LOW
WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.
15 failed tests
INFRA LOW
🆕 New issue
Full AI Analysis

TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.

bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.

That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)

That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches

On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)

bc



Suite: bc (3 failures)


Suite TL;DR: All 3 bc ClockSync tests fail at the same step — GetProfileLogID cannot find the regex Ptp4lConf: #profile: ...message_tag [...] in the linuxptp-daemon container logs, because this log pattern only appears on a second profile application and the daemon had been stable for ~2 hours since cluster setup with no re-apply.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: GetProfileLogID regex never matches — daemon log pattern requires second profile apply (affects: all 3 tests)
• Evidence:

```

[Jun 26 04:03:18.197][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 1 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:04:28.470][ptphelper.go: 123] GetProfileLogID attempt 2 failed for test-bc-master1_test-bc-master1: could not get any profile line, ...context deadline exceeded, retrying...

[Jun 26 04:05:39.315][ptptesthelper.go: 68] GetClockIDForeign retry due to err: could not get any profile line, err=could not find regex in log stream for openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon-nqmpf container=linuxptp-daemon-container, err=error reading from stream: context deadline exceeded

```

Failure message (Test 1): Timed out after 202.124s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-bc-master1


Failure message (Test 2): Timed out after 202.701s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig test-slave1 (pod linuxptp-daemon-2vqqd)


Failure message (Test 3): Timed out after 201.746s. Timeout to get foreign clock ID for ptpconfig temp / err=timedout waiting for matches


Cluster setup completed at 02:06:48; ClockSync tests started at 04:02:12 (~1h55m later). The daemon applied profiles ONCE at setup startup and was stable since then.


• Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. Test 3 (higher priority profile) also creates a brand-new temp profile that is applied exactly once, so the same structural mismatch applies.

• Breaking PR/Commit: The structural flaw is in GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) itself — no single commit is the direct trigger. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)


TL;DR: 13 of 15 failures are a single test flake — a log-rotation race that voids GetProfileLogID; 2 are a WPC T-GM holdover hardware failure.



Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). Root is in the WPC DPLL firmware or the ts2phc_parser.go:162-163 holdover-detection logic, not in the test itself.


Cross-Suite Patterns:


SuiteFailuresRoot Cause
bc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualfollower3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbc3Log rotation (GetProfileLogID)
dualnicbcha2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
oc2Log rotation (GetProfileLogID) — same 206–218 s signature
tgm2WPC T-GM holdover hardware/firmware failure

All five non-tgm suites share an identical root cause and near-identical failure durations. The dualnicbcha and oc per-suite analyses characterize the symptom as "LOCKED state timeout," but the timing signature (206–218 s, 287–298 s) and the GetProfileLogID call stack are identical to bc/dualfollower/dualnicbc. tgm is the only suite with a distinct, hardware-level root cause.



Actionable Recommendations:


1. Fix GetProfileLogID to not depend on rotated startup logs. Instead of parsing the one-shot printNodeProfile startup line, query the current PTP configuration state directly — either from a /metrics endpoint, the PtpConfig CR status, or by triggering a daemon profile-reload (e.g., a no-op annotation update) immediately before the test assertion window. This single fix eliminates 13/15 failures.

2. Investigate the WPC DPLL holdover firmware behavior on cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com. Capture ts2phc raw log output during GNSS disconnect to determine whether the DPLL hardware never enters holdover or enters and exits it within milliseconds. If the latter, check whether the DPLL holdover duration register is being set correctly from ts2phc.holdover config and whether a firmware update is available for the WPC card.

3. Add a canary assertion for daemon log reachability before ClockSync tests. A short pre-check that confirms GetPodLogsRegex can read at least one log line from the daemon container (not the specific startup pattern) would convert a silent 206 s timeout into a fast, labeled skip/infra-failure, making future regressions easier to triage.


Overall Issue Class: test_flake (13/15); infra_other (2/15 tgm)


Overall Severity: MEDIUM — the dominant failure mode is a pre-existing, deterministic time-dependent flake (not a code regression); the tgm failures indicate a real hardware/firmware problem on one lab node but affect only 2 tests.




Per-Suite Details



bc

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)

Root Cause Groups:


• 13/15 failures — GetProfileLogID log-rotation timeout (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc, dualnicbcha, oc): BasicClockSyncCheck → GetClockIDForeign → GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) opens a Follow: true log stream and searches for (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: (.|\n)?message_tag \[.*\]. That regex only matches a line emitted once by printNodeProfile at daemon startup. Cluster setup finished at ~02:06; ClockSync tests ran at ~04:00–06:00 (~2–4 h later). By then CRI-O log rotation had cycled the startup entry out of the K8s-served log buffer. The snapshot read yields nothing (~26 s); the follow-stream times out after 180 s — producing the observed ~206–218 s failure duration across every suite. The "Can provide a profile with higher priority" variant adds ~60–90 s of profile-apply wait before hitting the same timeout (yielding ~287–298 s).

• 2/15 failures — WPC T-GM DPLL holdover not sustained (tgm): After GNSS disconnect, ts2phc either never emits the "holdover" log token (test 1 — 3+ min of retries, class stays at 6) or emits class-7 followed 7 ms later by class-248 (test 2 — DPLL flips through holdover and drops to FREERUN before the test can assert it). • Root Cause: GetProfileLogID (ptphelper.go:69) uses the regex (?m).?Ptp4lConf: #profile: %s(.|\n)?message_tag \[(.)\]. This pattern is only logged by printNodeProfile (daemon.go:893) when nodeProfile.Ptp4lConf already contains the RENDERED config (i.e., after configInput = configOutput at daemon.go:1119 has updated the pointer's string on a PREVIOUS apply). On the very first profile application, printNodeProfile logs the RAW CRD config which has NO #profile: header and NO message_tag line — both are added only by RenderPtp4lConf() / ExtendGlobalSection(). Since the daemon applied profiles once at cluster-setup time (~2 hours before tests) and never re-applied them, neither Pass 1 (existing logs) nor Pass 2 (follow stream) in GetPodLogsRegex (pods.go:214) can find the pattern. The two-pass GetPodLogsRegex was introduced/modified in commits 0f98a60 and 9c1bc7f; 41f3dd9 (May 11 2026) changed the initial snapshot timeout from 3 min to 1 min but is not the root regression. The underlying issue — that Ptp4lConf: #profile: only appears in logs on the 2nd+ apply — has existed as a latent flake since GetProfileLogID was introduced. Unknown PR for the original introduction; the relevant release branch is release-4.21 in github.com/openshift/ptp-operator.

• Is it a flake? Yes — the test passes when something triggers a second profile application between cluster setup and the ClockSync test window (e.g., operator config reconciliation, hardware config event, daemon restart). In this run, ~2 hours of stability after setup meant no re-apply occurred.

• Suggested Fix: In GetProfileLogID, instead of relying on the Ptp4lConf: log (which requires a second apply), read the ptp4l config file directly from the pod filesystem (/var/run/ptp4l.0.config)



TL;DR: WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: DPLL fails to reach/maintain clock class 7 (HOLDOVER) after GNSS loss (affects Tests: both)
• Evidence:

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

Matched line: [ptp4l 248] → GNSS disabled → Clock class not yet 7, retrying... (×32+)

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)


Test 2 — class 7 transient, immediately overtaken by 248:

PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:6], map[metric:7], map[metric:248]

GnssStateChange Event recieved map[metric:0 notification:FAILURE-NOFIX]

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

```

• Root Cause: After GNSS signal loss on node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, the WPC hardware DPLL transitions directly from LOCKED to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining HOLDOVER (class 7). In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the upstream sync PR #613 (0459bc0, CNF-24996/OCPBUGS-90708, merged 2026-06-23) was an upstream sync to the main branch and does not directly affect the release-4.22 branch under test; no code regression identified.
• Is it a flake? Yes — test 2 shows holdover does occur but transiently; this is a hardware-timing sensitivity, consistent with intermittent failures when DPLL holdover duration is at the margins.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.
• Affected tests:
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — both failures share the same root cause: DPLL on cnfdf32 not sustaining HOLDOVER state long enough after GNSS loss. Test 1 never sees class 7 at all; test 2 sees it transiently. Both are direct manifestations of the same abnormally short holdover window on this hardware.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.22 (nightly-2026-06-23-164229) — both failures in the [tgm-serial] suite only; all other suites (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc) show 0 failures and passed cleanly.


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/cloud-event-proxy:4.22


Overall Issue Class: test_flake


Overall Severity: LOW




TL;DR: WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: DPLL fails to reach/maintain clock class 7 (HOLDOVER) after GNSS loss (affects Tests: both)

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)


Test 2 — class 7 transient, immediately overtaken by 248:

PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:6], map[metric:7], map[metric:248]

GnssStateChange Event recieved map[metric:0 notification:FAILURE-NOFIX]

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the upstream sync PR #613 (0459bc0, CNF-24996/OCPBUGS-90708, merged 2026-06-23) was an upstream sync to the main branch and does not directly affect the release-4.22 branch under test; no code regression identified.
• Is it a flake? Yes — test 2 shows holdover does occur but transiently; this is a hardware-timing sensitivity, consistent with intermittent failures when DPLL holdover duration is at the margins.
• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.

• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM GNSS signal loss tests Testing WPC T-GM holdover through connection loss [Serial]
• [It] [tgm-serial] PTP e2e tests WPC GM Events verification (V2) Testing T-GM Events on GNSS reboot and recovery [Serial]


Relation Between Groups: Single group — both failures share the same root cause: DPLL on cnfdf32 not sustaining HOLDOVER state long enough after GNSS loss. Test 1 never sees class 7 at all; test 2 sees it transiently. Both are direct manifestations of the same abnormally short holdover window on this hardware.


Per-Version Notes: OCP 4.22 (nightly-2026-06-23-164229) — both failures in the [tgm-serial] suite only; all other suites (bc, dualfollower, dualnicbc) show 0 failures and passed cleanly.


Affected Images: image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/ptp-operator:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/linuxptp-daemon:4.22, image-registry.openshift-image-registry.svc:5000/openshift-ptp/cloud-event-proxy:4.22

Overall Severity: LOW

2026-07-02T00:00
❌ failure4.224.22-e2e-telco5g-ptp268mtest_flake` (tgm: infra/hardware flake) + `test_regression` (bc and dualnicbcha: `main` test-code issues) MEDIUM
Two test-code regressions and one GNSS hardware flake caused all 5 failures across bc, dualnicbcha, and tgm suites.
3 failed tests

TL;DR: WPC T-GM node cnfdf32 DPLL never entered HOLDOVER (clock class 7) after GNSS coldboot.


Root Cause Groups:


• Group 1: DPLL fails to reach/maintain clock class 7 (HOLDOVER) after GNSS loss (affects Tests: both)
• Evidence:

```

Test 1 — DPLL stuck at FREERUN for full 200s after GNSS disabled:

Matched line: [ptp4l 248] → GNSS disabled → Clock class not yet 7, retrying... (×32+)

→ GNSS enabled → [FAILED] Timed out waiting for clock class 7 (ptp.go:3802, 3m20s elapsed)


Test 2 — class 7 transient, immediately overtaken by 248:

PtpClockClassChange Event recieved map[metric:6], map[metric:7], map[metric:248]

GnssStateChange Event recieved map[metric:0 notification:FAILURE-NOFIX]

→ STEP: Verifying ClockClass value to 7 → [FAILED] expected a metric [7.000000] but got map[metric:248] (ptp.go:3898)

```

• Root Cause: After GNSS signal loss on node cnfdf32.telco5gran.eng.rdu2.redhat.com, the WPC hardware DPLL transitions directly from LOCKED to FREERUN (class 248) without sustaining HOLDOVER (class 7). In pkg/event/event.go, clock class 7 requires dpllState == PTP_HOLDOVER; since the DPLL reports FREERUN immediately, that path is never taken. In test 2, HOLDOVER was briefly entered (class 7 visible in the event stream) but exhausted faster than the metric-polling step could verify it — confirming the DPLL holdover window on this hardware is abnormally short.
• Breaking PR/Commit: Unknown — the upstream sync PR #613 (0459bc0, CNF-24996/OCPBUGS-90708, merged 2026-06-23) was an upstream sync to the main branch and does not directly affect the release-4.22 branch under test; no code regression identified.

• Suggested Fix: Investigate DPLL holdover configuration on cnfdf32 (check LocalHoldoverTimeout configured for the WPC hardware); if hardware is degraded or misconfigured for holdover, reprovisioning or reconfiguring the node should resolve it. Retry the job to confirm transience.
